channel.title: Nicotine Science and Policy
channel.description: Daily digest of views, debates and discussions on nicotine science, policy, regulation and advocacy.

item.title: Michael Bloomberg’s war on vaping puts criminals over consumers
item.description: Former New York MayorMichael Bloomberghas taken to one of his own news channels to deliver a broadside againstflavored vaping products. This diatribe is remarkable for its flagrant contradictions, staggering lack of self-awareness, and complete lack of understanding about harm reduction. Bloomberg warns that the Food and Drug Administration’srecent authorizationof flavored vaping products will “prove deadly for kids.” He paints a lurid picture of an America supposedly on the verge of societal collapse, with predatory companies handing out mango-flavored addiction to children on every street corner. [...] (Published on June 05, 2026bywashingtonexaminer.com)

item.title: Chinese-made ‘smart vapes’ could be used to spy on Brits through their mobile phones, Labour MP warns
item.description: Chinese-made smart vapes could pose a security threat by potentially spying on Brits through their phones, warns Labour MP Graeme Downie. Concerns include data harvesting, tracking movements, and cyber attacks. US senators have also raised alarms about these devices, which feature Bluetooth and app connectivity. The Coalition on Secure Technology urges the UK to ban Chinese tech in vapes, citing risks of data exploitation by hostile actors. The group calls for government action to assess security risks of connected vaping devices and strengthen import checks. The NCSC advises caution with internet-connected devices and emphasizes the importance of strong cybersecurity measures. (Published on June 05, 2026bythesun.co.uk)

item.title: Australia's tobacco black market branded ‘global laughing stock’ as illegal rates trump dozens of countries
item.description: Australia's severe tobacco black market problem is termed a "total failure of policy" after Australian Bureau of Statistics revealed a 40% increase in national nicotine consumption from 2017 to 2025, with illicit products making up 80% of tobacco use. This far surpasses similar data from European nations, Canada, and New Zealand. Former police detective Rohan Pike criticized public health advocates, stating that Australia's anti-tobacco policies are flawed and ineffective, and suggested a fresh approach. KPMG reports show varying rates of illegal tobacco consumption in European markets, Canada, and Latin America, emphasizing the prevalence of contraband products globally. FTI Consulting noted a significant 27.2% of illicit tobacco consumption in New Zealand in 2024. (Published on June 05, 2026byskynews.com.au)
